diagrams.title: LinearLayout Structure - Horizontal and Vertical
diagrams.description: Shows how LinearLayout arranges child views in a single line, either horizontally (left-to-right) or vertically (top-to-bottom)
elements: Parent LinearLayout container with orientation attribute
Child View 1 (Button/TextView)
Child View 2 (EditText)
Child View 3 (Button)
Arrows showing flow direction: horizontal → or vertical ↓
Weight attribute visualization showing proportional sizing
Gravity attribute showing alignment options
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how LinearLayout organizes UI elements sequentially and understand the importance of orientation, weight, and gravity attributes for controlling element placement and sizing.

diagrams.title: AbsoluteLayout Positioning System
diagrams.description: Demonstrates how AbsoluteLayout uses exact X-Y coordinates to position child views within the container
elements: Parent AbsoluteLayout container with defined width/height
Child View 1 positioned at (x=10, y=20)
Child View 2 positioned at (x=100, y=50)
Coordinate grid overlay showing X-axis and Y-axis
Dotted lines showing absolute positions
Overlap zone where views can stack on each other
diagrams.purpose: Students understand absolute positioning concepts, learn how to use android:layout_x and android:layout_y attributes, and recognize the trade-offs of using absolute coordinates versus relative layouts.

diagrams.title: FrameLayout Overlay Concept
diagrams.description: Illustrates how FrameLayout stacks child views on top of each other, useful for overlays and layered UIs
elements: Parent FrameLayout container filling entire screen area
Base layer: Background ImageView
Middle layer: TextView with semi-transparent background
Top layer: ProgressBar or Loading indicator
Z-ordering arrow showing which view appears on top
match_parent attributes demonstrating frame filling
diagrams.purpose: Students learn how FrameLayout manages overlapping views, understands the concept of view stacking order (last child on top), and sees practical applications like image overlays and loading indicators.

diagrams.title: Android View Hierarchy Tree
diagrams.description: Shows the hierarchical relationship between Activity, ViewGroups, and Views in Android UI
elements: Activity (root node)
LinearLayout as parent (ViewGroup)
Nested RelativeLayout child (ViewGroup)
Individual Views (TextView, Button, EditText) as leaf nodes
Parent-child relationship arrows
XML hierarchy indentation showing nesting levels
diagrams.purpose: Students understand how Android layouts form a tree structure, learn the difference between ViewGroups (containers) and Views (UI elements), and see how nested layouts affect performance.

diagrams.title: Layout Attributes Comparison Block Diagram
diagrams.description: Visual representation of key attributes for each layout type and their effects
elements: LinearLayout box: orientation, gravity, layout_weight, weightSum
AbsoluteLayout box: layout_x, layout_y, layout_width, layout_height
FrameLayout box: layout_gravity, foreground, foregroundGravity
Color-coded attribute categories (positioning, sizing, alignment)
Arrows connecting attributes to their visual effects
diagrams.purpose: Students quickly reference the specific attributes available for each layout type and understand which attributes control positioning, sizing, and visual alignment.

charts.title: Layout Managers Comparison Table
charts.content: Comprehensive comparison of LinearLayout, AbsoluteLayout, and FrameLayout: LinearLayout arranges elements in single direction (horizontal/vertical), uses weight for proportional sizing, simple and most commonly used; AbsoluteLayout uses exact X-Y coordinates, allows overlap, deprecated in favor of RelativeLayout; FrameLayout stacks views in Z-order, efficient for single-child or overlay scenarios, fills entire parent space by default.

charts.title: Choosing the Right Layout Flowchart
charts.content: Decision tree for selecting layout: Start → Need elements in row/column? → Yes → Use LinearLayout; No → Need exact positioning? → Yes → Use AbsoluteLayout (or ConstraintLayout); No → Need view stacking/overlays? → Yes → Use FrameLayout; No → Consider RelativeLayout or ConstraintLayout.

charts.title: Common Attributes Reference Table
charts.content: Layout attributes table showing: layout_width (match_parent, wrap_content, dp values), layout_height (match_parent, wrap_content, dp values), orientation (horizontal, vertical), gravity (top, bottom, center, left, right, center_horizontal, center_vertical), layout_weight (numeric values for proportional sizing), padding vs margin differences.

charts.title: ViewGroup Classification Chart
charts.content: Classification of Android Layout Managers into categories: Linear Layouts (LinearLayout, Weight), Relative Layouts (RelativeLayout, ConstraintLayout), Frame-based (FrameLayout), Table-based (TableLayout, GridLayout). Shows inheritance from ViewGroup parent class.

branches: LinearLayout - Sequential arrangement, horizontal/vertical, weight support
AbsoluteLayout - Absolute X-Y positioning, coordinate-based, allows overlap
Common Attributes - layout_width, layout_height, padding, margin
View Hierarchy - Parent-child relationships, nesting impact
Selection Criteria - Use case, device independence, complexity
mindMaps.purpose: Provides comprehensive overview connecting all three primary layout managers, their unique characteristics, shared attributes, and guides students on when to use each type for effective Android UI design.

branches: Gravity - Controls child alignment within container
Layout Weight - Proportional sizing with weightSum
Performance - Simpler than RelativeLayout, faster rendering
Common Use Cases - Forms, lists, navigation bars, button groups
mindMaps.purpose: Helps students master LinearLayout in depth as the most frequently used layout manager, reinforcing key concepts through structured concept mapping.
